data.title: March CPI Just Flipped The Inflation Debate
data.description:
The March CPI release is adding fuel to a fragile ceasefire rally.

Headline inflation landed exactly where economists expected (3.2% y/y and 0.9% m/m), but core CPI, the figure that matters more for the Fed's monetary policy, came in softer than expected (2.6% y/y vs. 2.7% y/y consensus).

Despite the Strait of Hormuz still remaining largely closed, with post-ceasefire transits in the low teens or high single-digit figures depending on the source you are reading, I am increasingly more bullish on the broader markets.

In fact, I already loaded the truck yesterday, and I may add even more stocks to my portfolio today.

Why?

Simply because the market is still overly pessimistic about the Fed's monetary policy. Despite the encouraging March CPI report, economists still believe in zero cuts this year.

It is my view that these experts are dead wrong, although I am not singing victory yet.

If April and May CPI prints rhyme with the March report, I see a real chance of the first rate cut as soon as June this year. Below, I explain why.

The Long-Awaited March CPI Report

I’m the first to admit that over the past few weeks, I’ve been checking the Cleveland Fed’s inflation nowcast more than I want to recognize (daily, sometimes, even twice a day as new data came in).

Prior to the BLS March CPI release, the Cleveland Fed nowcast for headline CPI was 0.84% m/m and 3.25% y/y, with core CPI at 0.20% m/m and 2.60% y/y. The Reuters consensus was 0.9% m/m and 3.3% y/y for headline CPI and 0.3% m/m and 2.7% y/y for core CPI.

Well, the actual numbers from the Bureau of Labor Statistics just came in, and it’s a big relief to see them (especially because I loaded up the truck yesterday after putting my cash back to work).

The headline CPI was up 0.9% m/m and 3.3% y/y, while core CPI increased 0.2% m/m and 2.6% y/y.

In other words, the CPI matched the consensus on the headline, but the more important (from an interest rate policy perspective) core CPI came in softer than the consensus anticipated.

As I'm writing, the market's reaction to this read was mostly positive, except for the Dow Jones, as one would expect due to the predominance of non-tech and non-AI growth stock exposure.

From a sector perspective, I see more sectors today in the green than in the red, which is a good sign after such an important economic release.

Coming back to the March CPI, the main focus was on whether energy costs were spilling over into other areas. They didn't, mostly.

Food was flat overall, which is a big relief:

The index for food was unchanged over the month as the index for food away from home rose 0.2 percent, while the index for food at home fell 0.2 percent.

To me, this suggests that the higher fuel prices are largely absorbed by corporations and are not passed on to consumers.

Speaking of fuel prices, gasoline alone accounted for nearly 75% of the monthly increase in the headline CPI. To be specific, energy was up 10.9% on the month, with gasoline up 21.2% and fuel oil up 30.7%.

On the oil side, Brent is still hovering in the mid-$90s level, as the ceasefire looks very fragile and the transits through the Strait of Hormuz appear to be conditional on Iran's approval, with some sources suggesting there are passage fees for tankers.

Trump didn't seem to be happy about this:

By the end of March, the U.S. average retail gasoline price was sitting at $3.99/gallon, a 55% increase from Jan 2025.

Notably, jet fuel was up 84% in the same timeframe. Therefore, I was not surprised to see some elements within the core CPI ticking up in March.

Specifically, airline fares increased by 2.7%, and transportation services rose by 0.6% in March, which suggests that in some pockets of the market, there has been an early oil pass-through to consumers.

That said, I am not sweating about that increase. Why?

Core goods, excluding food and energy, rose just 0.1%. In other words, the fear that corporations are broadly passing through the transportation costs to consumers (outside of a few pockets in the market) is overblown, at least for now.

Am I going to pop a bottle of champagne tonight after this CPI report?

You bet I am, despite the fact that core CPI is still above the Fed's 2% target. The reason I am optimistic is largely that the print didn't show a generalized surge in prices across the basket.

Therefore, if the April/May CPI shows a similar result, likely incoming Fed Chair Kevin Warsh will have one more reason to (aggressively) cut interest rates, on top of the one below:

Bear in mind that Trump wants interest rates as low as 1%. I don't think he will get that any time soon, but directionally, I strongly believe the Fed fund rate will be down this year.

Based on the CME Group survey results below, I believe I am mostly alone with my dovish view, which presents a strong catalyst for the second half of the year.

Back to inflation, there are still more secondary effects to look for in the upcoming months, particularly for items exposed to jet fuel, diesel, freight, fertilizer, plastics, and other transport-sensitive costs.

So I don't think we are out of the woods yet. However, I don't expect new highs for oil prices, and I am increasingly leaning toward the view that the lows in the S&P 500 are behind us.

Bottom Line

I’m now more confident that the broader markets have bottomed, as the Hormuz blockade is not showing signs of significantly driving up core inflation.

As seen above, we are just 2.5% from new highs, which reflects the market confidence through these uncertain times.

First, I say confidence, and not arrogance, because, as we learned last year with Trump's tariffs, things are always less impactful than what the headlines and "experts" say.

Second, I say uncertain because, from what I'm reading, there have been no oil tankers traversing the Strait of Hormuz. It appears that the only post-ceasefire passages were done by dry bulk and container ships, with numbers in the high single digits to low teens. That's an improvement, but still below the 138 per day before the conflict began.

As Sultan Al Jaber, chief executive officer of Abu Dhabi National Oil Co., puts it, the Strait of Hormuz is still not open.

That said, the market is looking past the noise as Vice President JD Vance heads to Islamabad. I would keep a close eye on what happens this Sunday in Pakistan, as I believe the end of the conflict may be in sight.

data.title: Atmus Filtration Technologies' Surge Isn't A Good Reason To Turn Optimistic
data.description:
Back in September of last year, I decided to rate Atmus Filtration Technologies Inc. (ATMU) a "Hold." I acknowledged that the business was a solid operator. But I was not enthusiastic about the price. Despite continued growth on the top and bottom lines, the stock looked priced at levels that were more in the fair value range than in the undervalued range. The market has since disagreed with my assessment, pushing shares up 29.9% while the S&P 500 is down 0.5%. This return disparity is great for its investors but clearly a challenge to my thesis. So as part of my analysis, I decided to dig deeper again and see whether my assessment should still be viewed as credible. Although the company is certainly trading on the cheaper end of the spectrum compared to other similar firms, it is priced at levels that are difficult to justify if your goal is to describe it as undervalued. So at the risk that the market might continue to move against my call, I believe that maintaining it as a "Hold" makes sense here.

Checking In On Atmus Filtration Technologies

If you are new to Atmus Filtration Technologies, it would be helpful to understand what the company is and what it does. At its core, the company operates as a player in the filtration products space. It caters to on-highway commercial vehicles, off-highway agricultural vehicles, construction equipment, mining equipment, power generation vehicles, and more. To many investors, this might not seem all that exciting a space to play in. But for me, it is intriguing. This is especially true when you consider that the business has a couple of things going forward.

Although it is a U.S.-centric enterprise, with revenue from the United States and Canada accounting for 58% of sales last year, it gets large chunks of revenue from Latin America, Europe, the Middle East, Africa, and the Asia Pacific region of the world. The other thing going for it is that it is not really exposed all that much to the OEM space. Instead, it has positioned itself as a player in the aftermarket. About 87% of sales come from that space. That is appealing to me on its own because the aftermarket industry has a tendency to be less susceptible to broader economic challenges.

The newest data that investors have right now covers through the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the company was $446.6 million. That was up soundly from the $406.7 million that the company reported a year earlier. Management attributed this 9.8% increase to a mixture of things, including higher prices, higher volumes, and favorable foreign currency fluctuations. Higher prices added about 5% to the top line for the company. Higher volumes added 4%. And foreign currency fluctuations aided to the tune of 1%. Management did provide additional details about this and their latest earnings release. According to management, one contributor to the upside that the business saw was the firm's launch of its NanoNet N3 filtration media, which won a very prestigious award in the industry. On top of this, the firm is continuing to push into the aftermarket space since it views that as a cornerstone of its growth potential.

As revenue increased, profitability improved also. Net income expanded from $40.1 million to $48 million. It is worth noting that certain manufacturing costs decreased for the company, which worked well when paired with the higher prices and increased volumes. Other profitability metrics grew similarly. Operating cash flow, for starters, more than doubled from $20 million to $47.5 million. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a surge from $43.7 million to $83.6 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $77.7 million to $85.2 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024. Thanks to $51.9 million of increased volumes and $50 million of higher prices, both of which eclipsed the $7.3 million negative impact associated with foreign currency fluctuations, revenue shot up from $1.67 billion to $1.76 billion. Not surprisingly, overall profitability expanded also. In fact, as the company's gross profit margin grew from 27.7% to 28.2%, even at a time when tariffs cost the business $36.2 million directly, overall net income expanded from $185.6 million to $207.4 million. Other profitability metrics rose as well, as the aforementioned chart illustrates. In fact, there's not a single profitability metric that I looked at that showed the business worsening.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management has provided some interesting guidance. They expect revenue to come in at between $1.945 billion and $2.015 billion. At the midpoint, this would be 12.2% above the $1.76 billion that the company reported last year. However, it would be a mistake to overstate how much of this growth will be organic. You see, back in November of last year, management announced that the company was acquiring Koch Filter Corporation, a producer of essential air filtration systems for certain markets like the industrial and commercial HVAC space, data centers, power generation, and other related industries. Despite the business bringing in annual revenue of only $156 million, management ended up paying $450 million in cash for the enterprise.

This works out to an EV-to-EBITDA multiple of about 13.9. But when you factor in the present value of expected tax benefits from the transaction, which alone reduces the price of the acquisition to $395 million on a net basis, and you factor in cost synergies that management is forecasting, the multiple lowers to 10.9. In a moment, you will see that this is actually quite low considering how shares of Atmus Filtration Technologies are trading at the moment. That deal ultimately closed in early January. And guidance for 2026 does incorporate it. However, what does complicate matters is the fact that we need to factor in, when evaluating the company on a comparable basis, what owning Koch Filter Corporation would have meant for the enterprise for 2025.

In the table above, you can see, factoring in the data that management provided as well as reasonable estimates for other items, how much in EBITDA, net profits, and operating cash flow Koch Filter Corporation should bring to Atmus Filtration Technologies on an annual basis. I used the same tax rate that the company had for 2025. For depreciation, depletion, and amortization, I assumed that the amount would equal the same percent of revenue that Atmus Filtration Technologies had for last year. And for the interest rate on the $395 million in net debt, I used a reading of 5.2% to reflect the current credit facility debt that is paying based on SOFR plus a range of between 1.25% and 1.75% on top of that.

Using the adjusted figures for 2025, then, we can see in the chart above how shares of Atmus Filtration Technologies are priced at the moment. These certainly aren't bad multiples. But I wouldn't say that they put the company in the cheap category either. Relative to other similar firms, of course, the stock is certainly on the cheaper end of the spectrum. And that gives me a good reason to appreciate the stance that those who are bullish about the business might have on it. But as somebody who prioritizes absolute value as opposed to relative value, I put more weight on the absolute value side of the equation.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the market has been a little overly optimistic regarding Atmus Filtration Technologies. The company is certainly a great firm. And it is cheaper compared to some other similar enterprises. But on the whole, the stock is not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook in my view. So for now, I am maintaining it as a "Hold" candidate.

data.title: The Magnificent 7 Broke - That's Exactly Why I'm Buying
data.description:
Let me give you a quick image: a rock star arriving late on stage, everyone waiting for her, everyone knows her, everyone continues to listen and watch, but they can no longer ignite the same enthusiasm as before. That's how I see the Magnificent Seven, who seem to have gone from being untouchable, as I often say, to mere mortals. Software was marketed with the impression that AI would destroy their business model overnight. Each additional $1 billion dollar added into CapEx was viewed by investors as more of a risk than an investment.

Obviously, the war provided the perfect catalyst for correction and increased uncertainty in the markets. As a result, it made everything more violent. However, according to me, the primary issue was never the War. The issue was always Valuation. As stated previously in other articles on Seeking Alpha, the market refused to fall, despite stretched multiples and a less-than-reassuring news flow. Possibly it was retail support, possibly it was typical Inertia associated with major trends, or possibly there were other forces at work. What is evident is that the markets needed to take a breath. Finally, the market took a breath.

The real question is not whether sentiment has decreased further. Clearly that has happened. The real question is whether this fall has already created a good buying opportunity or if it is too soon to put money back into the industry.

For myself, I had already unloaded some of my tech exposure some time ago, and those who follow my analyses know this. Therefore, today the issue is not chasing/rushing to get back into this area, but rather identifying whether we are currently experiencing a normal phase of valuation decompression or if it signifies a more critical event. Since these periods of time (when markets move from euphoria to rejection) typically provide the most favorable opportunities, those who can differentiate between fear and a deterioration in fundamental values will be able to capitalize on them.

Tech's Weaknesses

Tech has shown unusual dynamics so far in 2026. Since the beginning of the year, the seven “Magnificent” have underperformed versus the overall market. The S&P 500 (SP500) index has traded relatively flat.

However, rather than viewing this performance as a mass decline, I view this as a dispersion within the group. While some companies have experienced significant declines, other companies are performing well. These two different areas will result in a very different perspective going forward. One could say investors are exiting the space. In my opinion, a better way to describe what is occurring is that investors are simply rethinking their expectations regarding what they expect from the space.

For several years prior to 2026, the tech space was essentially viewed as an asset-light area. High-margin growth, low incremental investments (i.e., capital expenditures), and good conversion of revenue to cash were all expected from tech businesses. The rise of artificial intelligence changed that expectation. Today, many large-cap companies in the space are forced to purchase, finance or construct production capacity such as servers, chips, data centers and/or energy. When an entity increases its capital intensity, the market views it through a completely new lens.

#1 CapEx

One of the major issues related to increasing capital intensity is free cash flow. Increasing capital expenditures (CAPEX) decreases free cash availability for near-term use by a company for dividend payments, buybacks and/or acquisitions. Additionally, increased CapEx increases a company’s risk associated with execution because only adequate future returns justify the current level of investment. Therefore, the market is switching from a “growth-at-all-costs” mentality to a far more conservative approach – i.e., measuring return on invested capital.

This change in focus creates great interest in how these companies are being valued today. Not because the AI thesis failed nor because innovation ended, but because virtually every segment of the tech space is being priced with a great deal more skepticism. It is when the market moves away from chasing a story and focuses on actual numbers that the best opportunities tend to surface.

#2 Duration

A second major concern facing the sector is duration. Because growth stocks are viewed as long-duration instruments, those sectors experience greater pressure during periods of rising inflation and/or higher real yields. Thus, geopolitical events matter significantly. For example, if an event such as a block of Hormuz occurs or an oil price shock results from a major conflict and/or disruption in supply, certain segments of the tech space tend to be negatively impacted due to perceived inflationary pressures. Conversely, should fear of recession prevail and real interest rates begin to drop, many of the same tech stocks which suffered losses during previous periods of fear may rapidly gain favor.

#3 Monetization

Thirdly, and equally importantly, there has been a credibility crisis on monetization. As late as 2024, simply mentioning "AI" would result in many stocks getting revalued. That’s now not sufficient for 2026. Markets want figures, not slogans; they want to know about unit economies, backlogs, pricing power, run-rates, and number of customers. Essentially, markets do not want to be told how great the future will be. They want to begin measuring the present.

#4 Dispersion

Lastly, there is a rotation within the sector itself. Perhaps the most interesting aspect of this story is that tech is not moving as one singular entity. Underneath, there is considerable dispersion amongst sub-sectors, with frequent rotations occurring amongst them. In certain times during this cycle, software will be selected for targeting due to its perception of vulnerability, replaceability, or exposure to margin compression. At the same time, infrastructure and semiconductor firms are reaping benefits as they are perceived as the ones "laying down the AI rails," not just trying to find ways to make money from it later on.

It may seem overly simplistic when considering the market is separating those that "create rails" from those that "sell tickets." However, such distinctions are critical at this time.

Therefore, the only seemingly obvious question is: Is this weakness an an ordinary repricing of assets (and thus a normal response to overvaluation), or does this represent the notion that AI will ultimately become so expensive relative to possible future earnings? I have formed my own opinion regarding this issue and will detail why below in this article.

AI Capex: Cost Today, Barrier Tomorrow

Bloomberg Intelligence estimates that the total capital expenditures for six major U.S. hyperscalers are expected to exceed $750 billion dollars in 2026, representing a +80% YoY increase after another record setting year in 2025. As shown below, the data illustrates the jump in scale and the trend of the costs being "stuck" at a high level in future years.

Investors may respond to such information in one of two fashions:

  1. There is a natural reaction to run, based on historical experience of "infrastructure booms," where those building out the infrastructure typically receive poor returns and those arriving late to the party reap the rewards. Examples include railroads, electrification and the Internet; they were all innovative technologies that provided initial returns to early adopters that were highly asymmetrical.
  2. An alternative response would be far more valuable to investors: "This is a barrier to entry, therefore I want to understand who will become indispensable." When capital expenditures grow at an exponential rate, some participants do benefit proportionately while others suffer disproportionately. The consequence is that the market tends toward increased concentration of market share among fewer dominant winners. Hyperscalers' increasing dominance in global data center capacity, as reported by Synergy Research Group, will lead to decreased direct management of their own infrastructure, resulting in even greater concentration of benefits within a smaller group of players and reduced distribution of remaining computing resources throughout the broader market.

While we now recognize that capex will continue to grow, we also need to identify where the capex will be growing most significantly. At this time, rather than merely focusing on capex growth rates, we should focus our efforts on identifying the beneficiaries of such growth.

The Business Case Studies

At this juncture, it's important to look at the business case studies. As previously stated, if one views infrastructure simply as an expense, then the market is rational to reduce valuation multiples; however, if one views the development of infrastructure as providing an incremental stream of income, then one could argue that the investment in capital expenditures represents a good choice.

Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN)

I previously wrote an article in which I argued that Amazon was a Buy. More specifically, based upon the letter sent by CEO Andy Jassy to shareholders, Amazon has reached an approximate annualized run rate of $15 billion related to artificial intelligence for the first quarter. Further, Amazon has consistently referenced AWS as continuing to accelerate during the most recent earnings calls. Moreover, Amazon has highlighted the growing role of its chip business (Graviton/Trainium). However, the primary issue remains “visibility,” and consider that a substantial amount of the planned capital expenditures through 2026 are likely to have existing commitments and the monetization of those expenditures is anticipated to occur primarily between 2027-28 (in a short-term or relatively short-term timeframe).

This type of statement, assuming that such statements regarding backlog, growth and margin performance are confirmed in subsequent quarterly reports, may alter the manner in which the marketplace views spending by a hyperscale company. No longer will spending be viewed solely as “burning cash” but instead will be viewed as “building now and harvesting later.”

Then, Amazon is not only committing capital expenditures to acquire technology from outside sources, but it is also developing and creating its own technology. This process alone currently generates over $20 billion each year in revenue and there exists the opportunity for additional growth should Amazon begins selling such technology to external parties. This too is relevant in how one interprets such expenditure. When a hyperscale company develops chips, platforms, etc. and then sells such products and/or services to third party customers, such expenditure is no longer solely a cost associated with developing such technologies. Instead, such expenditure can become a new source of revenue.

Meta Platforms, Inc.

Meta provides another example. Meta was quite specific in stating that its artificial intelligence strategy will necessitate significant investments. Specifically, Meta cited silicon and energy as two of the primary areas where investments will be necessary. However, Meta did not state that it intends to merely commit large amounts of capital. Rather, Meta indicated that it aims to create structures that will decrease the cost per gigawatt over time and increase the efficiency of such structures.

High barriers to entry

This represents the crux of my contention. In my view, the marketplace is at risk of misinterpreting the true implications of such expenditures. While such expenses currently frighten investors due to their impact on free cash flow and overall perceived risk profile, these same expenditures create very high barriers to entry. And when a company commits sufficient resources to hinder competitors’ ability to remain competitive, what appears to be excessive spending today can potentially become a major competitive advantage tomorrow.

The value does not lie exclusively in the artificial intelligence models themselves. Rather, the true value lies in the capability to design, develop and operate the systems required to support all elements of such models. And what may appear “too expensive” today due to the magnitude of spending on these projects may appear “affordable” tomorrow due to the increased leverage and capabilities created by having established these systems.

Valuations: Why I'm Still Bullish Now

I want to focus on the main message of the article. Are risks or opportunities developing? I maintain a positive outlook regarding technology stocks; especially, regarding the “Magnificent Seven” (with the exception of Tesla (TSLA), which I will never purchase at current valuations). My optimism is mainly due to the fact that prices fell and the risk/reward ratio has shifted in my favor.

Now that valuation considerations are beginning to influence the marketplace and this changes the outlook considerably. The Price-to-Earnings (P/E) multiple of Mag7 has declined dramatically since October. Their P/E had dropped approximately 25% (from about 35 times earnings to about 26 times earnings). The software reset was even more extreme with multiples falling from approximately 35 times earnings to around 20 times earnings. A drop in multiples alone is not enough to indicate whether the bottom has been hit. However, a decline in the price paid for each dollar of expected future earnings makes a substantial difference in terms of how well an investor performs. The lower price reduces the amount of perfect performance required by investors to obtain a satisfactory return. So, the risk/reward equation improves.

Another aspect that frequently gets overlooked is that these companies aren't investing in CapEx because they're forced to chase liquidity or defend a struggling business. Rather, these companies are investing in CapEx as a result of having the ability and desire to invest in their businesses, thereby improving their competitiveness and building longer term value. Therefore, for many of these companies, CapEx represents targeted capital allocation decisions rather than a sign of potential weakness.

The significance of this consideration increases further when evaluating the overall financial condition of the sector. Although the spread between borrowing costs widened and volatility increased, many of the largest publicly traded companies in the industry possess adequate levels of cash flow and operating margins to absorb this period of investment. With respect to financing, while some concern exists over the ability of companies to fund the AI build-out, these concerns do not seem as severe as implied by the "bubble" narrative surrounding AI. As an equity investor, the question is relatively straightforward: if a company has sufficient cash resources available to fund its investments, then capital expenditures become less of a liquidity issue and more a position-taking decision.

Additionally, because strategic investments made at lower valuation multiples can create an attractive asymmetrical profile for equity investors—i.e., the down-side already discounted into the valuation through compressions in valuation multiples, the up-side still exists should those investments generate positive returns—the distinction between liquidity and position-taking becomes increasingly relevant.

Therefore, I believe three specific questions need to be answered to fully appreciate this phase.

  1. Does this CapEx purchasing represent purchases of true scarcity — i.e., computing power, energy — or does it simply inflate the cost basis?
  2. Are there already identifiable signs of monetizing these investments through increasing revenue runs, order backlogs, accelerated business growth, etc.?
  3. In software products, is the competitive advantage related to distribution and integration, or is the product going to be viewed as just another feature with little differentiation?

If answers to these questions are favorable then last year's downturn appears to be an opportunity rather than a cautionary indicator.

A few other key factors and my conclusion

Bullish doesn't mean blind. I see four main risks.

  1. Firstly, capital expenditure (CapEx) needs a return on investment (ROI): If the company does have stable or non-accelerating core cloud growth and keeps increasing its capital expenditures (CapEx), then we will likely be looking at ROI questions that will compress multiples longer than the expected time period.
  2. Secondly, the companies need to manage their regulatory and energy-related challenges: Electricity demand and existing network constraint issues can be a barrier or structural cost, as well as political obstacles in establishing new Data Centers.
  3. Thirdly, there are legitimate concerns around potential disruption to SaaS companies: Certain categories of software-as-a-service (SaaS) may experience price pressures and/or slower growth. While the "SaaSpocalypse" is somewhat exaggerated, the underlying concern exists.
  4. Fourthly, there is always a technical and positioning risk associated with fast-paced rotation: Fast-paced rotations can produce drawdowns that cause even those who believe in the long-term value proposition to lose confidence.

Therefore, my conclusion is that yes, AI-related costs are affecting the current weakness in the tech sector, however, the weakness itself has created an even better opportunity for those who choose to invest in high-quality technology that has a realistic and long term horizon. The market wants proof. When an entire industry shifts from faith-based investing to evidence-based investing, two common phenomena occur: multiples decrease, and the chances of achieving positive returns in the future increase.

data.title: Silver: Clearest Sign That Structural Surpluses Will Continue
data.description:
There is an old saying that you are entitled to your own opinions, but you are not entitled to your own set of facts. We think an extension of that is that you are not entitled to your own made up math. Speculators in silver learned that the hard way as another set of predictions about a COMEX default turned out to be a dud. We are going to go over the predictions, why they failed and show you the biggest sign that the market is in a massive surplus.

COMEX Is Not The World

Imagine if your local grocery store ran out of milk. Then imagine if you assumed the world was running out of milk because of that. COMEX silver extrapolations have exactly been like that. While COMEX holds far more silver in relation to the world, than your grocery store holds milk, the concept is the same. Total world above-ground silver is close to 26-32 billion ounces.

COMEX registered inventory is about 80 million ounces. So we have 300-400 times more silver above ground. Trying to draw conclusions by movement in this tiny portion is like reading tea leaves in a desert storm. We got the most important part of the thesis correct. COMEX did not default in March and now in the middle April, still holds 78 million ounces of registered inventory. You cannot have default in a commodity where above-ground stocks are 60 years worth of current consumption. Sure prices can rise. Shorts can get squeezed. But default? Anyone selling you that fantasy was also selling an overpriced (if was over $0.00 it was overpriced) newsletter.

The Supply-Demand Dynamic

We apparently have to explain this again as pretty much no one gets it and everyone just plugs in Silver Institute's numbers as if they are gospel. Here is a table showing the "structural deficit" from Visual Capitalist.

The first thing you must ask is those numbers are true, where did all the deficit come from? If you spent more than you earned every single month for 5 years, either you had savings or you borrowed the rest. In the case of silver, we really can't borrow from a different dimension. So did we have savings? From our point, the answer is fairly simple. We do have 30 billion ounces of "savings" so a 95 million ounce deficit is a pretty easy fix. But unfortunately for the bulls, the silver story is not even that good. Primary consumption is still around 65% of the total supply, leaving 333 million excess ounces lying around. We define "consumption" literally how it sounds. Things that more or less permanently remove the silver from the equation. Yes, some of it is recycled, but since we are already counting recycling in our supply, we can count all consumption uses of silver as removing the silver completely.

To go from the 333 million surplus to a 95 million ounces deficit, you have to count the remaining categories. Note that each of those continue to add to the above-ground silver. But the most important thing one needs to understand here is that "bullion investment" is a incorrect category to add into the supply-demand balance. The reason is that if silver is not physically available from mine supply or recycling, how exactly does someone invest in bullion? The answer is actually fairly simple. That supply comes from existing investors letting go of their bullion at higher prices. What that means is if we see all these numbers stay the same for 2026 and Silver Institute reports 2026 bullion demand was 300 million ounces and then extrapolates that to a wildly bullish 200 million ounce plus deficit, it would only say that someone else was ready to sell those ounces at that price. More importantly, the driver of price will be the behavior of the herd holding those 30 billion ounces. Not the "deficit".

The Biggest Tell That There Is No Shortage

Sprott funds have redemption clauses and this extends to all their physical funds.

Minimum Redemption Amounts

Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS): A unitholder must have enough units to equate to one full-sized London Good Delivery bar (approximately 400 oz). Read more in the prospectus, Redemption of Trust Units for Physical Gold Bullion.

Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV): A unitholder must have enough units to equate to ten 1000 oz silver bars. Read more in the prospectus, Redemption of Trust Units for Physical Silver Bullion.

Sprott Physical Gold and Silver Trust (CEF): A unitholder must have at least 100,000 units. Read more in the prospectus, Redemption of Trust Units for Physical Gold Bullion and Silver Bullion.

Sprott Physical Platinum and Palladium Trust (SPPP): A unitholder must have a minimum of 25,000 trust units. Read more in the prospectus, Redemption of Trust Units for Physical Platinum and Palladium Bullion.

Source: Sprott

PSLV trades at a nice discount.

If there was really a shortage, the people who need the silver would buy it at 5% discount and redeem for actual physical holdings. This is actually the smoking gun and not the Shanghai Premiums which everyone kept suggesting as a precursor to sustainable triple digit prices. In fact, there has been no real notable redemption.

This to us is the clearest sign that there is a huge surplus and one that is likely to continue.

Verdict

Silver is in a structural surplus. Only 65% of total supply is consumed. The remaining hangs above ground and every now and then, people buy into wild stories about the deficit and push it into the stratosphere. Why would the price go up in a structural surplus? Well, just like with gold, the investment demand for the entire 30 billion ounces determines the price of each ounce. People can perceive it to be undervalued and create a buying mania. The market is modest-sized at $2.4 trillion (far smaller than gold) and certainly retail buying on leverage can amplify the moves. But there is no scarcity whatsoever. We have followed this story since 2000 and there were even articles suggesting that the fund ETF iShares Physical Bullion Trust (SLV) (SVR:CA), would completely rupture the market. The logic was that there was less than 1 billion ounces to be had and any systematic buying would push Silver well into the triple digits. But that is far from the case. If investors want to invest in silver, there are certainly good reasons. Just keep in mind the structural surplus.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Rithm Capital: Most Buy For The 10% Dividend, But The Upside Is The Thesis
data.description:
Rithm Capital: Coverage Initiation

This is the first time I have written about Rithm Capital. Three things led me to initiate my coverage: the struggles of the private credit sector (I have been dealing with it for some months), a business model transformation that is still not fully appreciated, and the case for a mispricing.

Let's start with the business model.

Rithm Capital At A Glance

Most people consider Rithm Capital (RITM) a mortgage REIT, but the truth is that the company has been actively working its way out of this definition, developing into a financial services holding company with four operating segments: Origination and Servicing, Investment Portfolio, Residential Transitional Lending, and Asset Management. This is why it describes itself as an asset management platform.

Origination and Service is the largest segment and operates through Newrez, the third largest US mortgage servicer and the fifth largest mortgage lender. At the end of FY25, Newrez reported pre-tax operating income of $1.1B and a 17% annualzied ROE on $5.9B of equity. Newrez has two ways to generate revenues: when it makes a loan, it then sells it to Fannie Mae or Freddie Mac at a price above the cost it had to face to originate it, creating a gain-on-sale income; when it manages loan pools on behalf of investors, it collects servicing fees. The first leg is cyclical, while the second generates recurring revenues. Here, we have to pay attention to the balance sheet and look at the mortgage servicing right (MSR) because, even though Rithm gives up the ownership of the loan, it still has the right to service the loan. However, since Rithm has to carry MSRs at fair value, every quarter it has to reprice the portfolio, reporting either gains or losses on the income statement. This is why we can see big swings in its earnings, even though they are caused by non-cash items. What changes when the MSR portfolio is repriced is the present value of the future cash flows that the portfolio is expected to generate. Particularly, interest rates have a big impact. When they decline, prepayments usually increase, which means that future cash flows shorten. This brings down the MSR fair value. There is a hedging strategy, but, in any case, the bottom line of the income statement takes a hit. Sometimes, the market prices this EPS volatility more than the strength of the business. At the end of FY25, Rithm reported $10.36B in MSRs, which means that a 10% repricing can create a swing worth $1B. To say it all, Rithm's CEO, Michael Nierenberg, said during the last earnings call that the company needs, at some point, to become a C-Corp, but that it needs to grow its asset management business a bit more. This would remove the EPS accounting noise, but we don't have a timeline yet. But careful investors could use this time before the conversion to be aware that Rithm's EPS carries this accounting problem.

We then have the Investment Portfolio, where we find non-agency residential-mortgage backed securities (RMBS), which are not guaranteed by the government, single-family rental properties, and loans to consumers. Residential Transition Lending is the segment whose operating brand is Genesis Capital, the second-largest U.S. residential transitional lender. Last year, it reported $4.8B in loans, a 33% YoY increase. Finally, we have Asset Management, operating mainly through Sculptor Capital Management and Crestline Management (acquired in December 2025). This is the segment on which the transition from an mREIT to an asset management platform hinges. Sculptor and Crestline manage around $56B and generated $627M at the end of FY25.

While all of the segments improved their results, the stock price, however, moved south. This is the first awkward situation that leads us to discuss Rithm's possible mispricing.

Key Metrics For Evaluating RITM

With mREITs, we would immediately go by book value per share. However, if you remember what we said about how the fair value of Rithm's MSRs swings, we can understand that the book value can fluctuate, too. For the same reason, the company's net income is not a very good starting point. It is much better to consider the company's earnings available for distribution (EAD), because it doesn't consider non-cash items. The EAD payout ratio is also important. AUM growth is another key metric for the Asset Management segment.

Rithm reported FY25 EAD of $1.28B, which, on a per share basis, translates into $2.35 (FY24: $2.10). This means that the EAD yield is around 23.65%, given the current share price of $9.93. The dividend yield is 10.09%, which means that the EAD payout ratio is 42.56%. This means that the dividend is extremely safe (we could even say it is almost conservative), since many other similar companies have payout ratios around 60% or even 65% (not to mention mREITS with payout ratios around 90%). If Rithm were to raise its dividend and align it with the industry, we would be talking about a dividend of roughly $1.50, which would be equal to a 15% yield.

So, if the company reports very strong numbers, why has the stock moved down, losing 9% YtD? It's true that in the last month the stock has rebounded from $8.70 to almost $10, so a repricing has been triggered. But we are still before very attractive metrics.

Interest rates also pose an interesting problem to Rithm. In fact, its two largest segments move inversely in response to interest rate changes. MSRs benefit when rates go up and do well in a higher-for-longer environment. However, Origination and Service benefits from lower interest rates because refinancing picks up speed. While this is an embedded hedging, it also confuses investors because it is hard to determine how Rithm should be properly repriced every time the Fed moves the needle. However, currently, markets are expecting no more than one rate cut this year, and the 30-yr fixed rate for mortgages is stable between 6% and 6.5%. This situation is actually one of the best for Rithm Capital's largest segment. In fact, those borrowers who took 3% mortgages during the pandemic don't need to refinance. This extends the MSR cash flow duration. If the Fed doesn't cut aggressively, these mortgages are likely to maintain their repayment schedule, with no need to prepay aggressively.

Speaking of financing, we have to deal with the opportunity created by the current situation in private credit. We have seen it move from being a niche alternative to a key part of many institutional portfolios. Specifically, the asset-backed security market is growing, and this is where Rithm works and thrives. It's true that private credit is currently under pressure, with delinquency rates slowly moving up and PIK becoming a larger issue. However, default rates are still well under control.

At the end of the day, we have to give credit where credit is due. Rithm Capital has built a platform that, at the end of FY25, had over $100B in investable assets. In particular, the acquisition of Crestline is a key milestone. On one side, direct-lending and NAV-based lending are growing. Secondly, it gives access to the insurance and reinsurance business. This means that the company now has access to stable capital that doesn't have to be redeemed or even gated. Clearly, Rithm comes out of this deal with a stronger structure.

We also have the recent Paramount acquisition, which exposes Rithm to office space in New York and San Francisco. While the return-to-office trend is growing, this new deal adds the operational risk that real estate always carries. Surely, in Q1 and Q2, I will monitor how Paramount's integration goes and how its contribution to the company's financials goes.

Valuation

There are many things that can be considered with such a diversified company, but I will leave other aspects for future articles. Right now, let's look at the valuation.

As said, a P/B ratio doesn't fully reflect the opportunity, even though a reading of 0.78 surely attracts us. If the stock were repriced to just 0.85x, which is more acceptable, we would have a stock price of $10.82, close to 10% upside from the current price. Let's also look at the asset management business. It reported FY25 revenues of $627M. If we use a conservative 20% EBITDA margin, we have more or less $125M in earnings. With a 12x multiple, this segment alone could be worth around $1.5B, which translates into $2.70 per share. This value is invisible when we consider the P/B ratio. If Crestline and Sculptor report steady AUM growth and margin expansion, the multiple could be moved up to 15x, which means that the segment could be worth around $5 per share, when we factor in its revenue growth, too. I think this is the real catalyst. If the market realizes the quality of this segment, the stock could move up to around $14, which represents an upside above 40%. Another big catalyst would be the conversion to a C-Corp. Investors would probably cheer, and this could add another $1 or $2 to the share price as a premium for earnings stability due to different accounting reasons.

Considering the stock has a 2.4x EAD coverage, and that the yield is above 10%, the question doesn't seem to be to me whether Rithm is undervalued, but when a catalyst comes out that helps the stock's multiple expansion. This is why I consider Rithm a convincing buy.

data.title: Replimune: Second CRL May Have Sealed RP1's Fate - It's Hard To See Positives
data.description:
News is breaking today that Replimune Group, Inc.'s (REPL) Biologics License Application ("BLA") requesting approval for its drug candidate RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec) in the indication of anti-PD-1 failed melanoma has been rejected by the FDA - the second time the FDA has rejected the drug for commercial approval.

At the time of writing, Replimune stock has fallen in value by nearly 20%, trading at a value of $4.76 per share - trading is currently halted. Stock is down >50% year-to-date, and nearly 85% on a five-year basis.

In this note, I'll provide some background and context to today's news and speculate about what it means for the company and its share price and valuation going forward.

Investment Overview - Background To Today's Decision

This is in fact my first time covering Replimune Group for Seeking Alpha, although I have frequently referenced the Woburn, Massachusetts-based biotech in my coverage of Iovance Biotherapeutics (IOVA) in the past.

As many readers will likely be aware, and as I noted in a March update on Iovance:

Iovance's lifileucel, brand name Amtagvi, is a commercially available therapy for advanced melanoma, having been approved in February 2024, and together with Proleukin (aldesleukin), an interleukin-2, or IL-2, product used in the Amtagvi treatment regimen, helped Iovance post a revenue figure of $264m in 2025.

it took far longer than expected to secure approval for Amtagvi than initially thought - the original biologics license application ("BLA") requesting FDA approval of the drug was submitted in 2022, but approval was not granted until 2024, testing Wall Street's patience.

Replimune's lead product is RP1, indicated for anti-PD-1 failed melanoma, i.e., patients did not respond to treatment with an immune checkpoint inhibitor - likely Merck's (MRK) Keytruda (pembrolizumab), or Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) Opdivo (nivolumab).

Replimune, like Iovance, has undergone a long and tortuous path towards an FDA approval, however its journey has ultimately not been completed - as per its fiscal year Q3 10-Q filing from early February:

In November 2024, we announced submission of our first Biologics License Application, or BLA, to the U.S. Food and Drug Administration, or the FDA, for RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec) in combination with nivolumab for the treatment of adult patients with advanced melanoma who have previously received an anti-PD-1 containing regimen, and that the FDA has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation for RP1 in combination with nivolumab in the same setting.

The submission was made under the accelerated approval pathway. The FDA accepted our BLA and granted priority review with a Prescription Drug User Fee Act, or PDUFA, goal date of July 22, 2025. On July 21, 2025 the FDA issued a complete response letter, or CRL, for the RP1 BLA for the treatment of advanced melanoma.

The dreaded CRL outlined the FDA's reasons for rejecting a application to approve a drug. Iovance notes:

The FDA stated in the CRL that it was unable to approve the application in its present form and that the IGNYTE trial was not considered to be an adequate and well-controlled clinical investigation that provided substantial evidence of effectiveness, including contribution of components. Furthermore, the FDA said the trial could not be adequately interpreted due to the heterogeneity of the patient population.

Clearly, Replimune was confident that it had addressed the FDA's concerns - during a fireside chat at this year's JP Morgan Healthcare conference in January, its CEO had told the audience:

So near term, we're looking forward to our approval, and we're already ready to execute on our commercial launch plans. That involves working very closely with the oncology and interventional radiology stakeholders. We've done a lot of work on now and ready to go. And we're also worked to simplify the logistics for our modalities so that we can enable next-day delivery and also stability at room temperature for our asset.

In its press release announcing the FDA acceptance of its resubmitted BLA, Replimune describes the mechanism of action ("MoA") of RP1 as follows:

RP1 (vusolimogene oderparepvec) is Replimune’s lead product candidate and is based on a proprietary strain of herpes simplex virus engineered and genetically armed with a fusogenic protein (GALV-GP R-) and GM-CSF, intended to maximize tumor killing potency, the immunogenicity of tumor cell death, and the activation of a systemic anti-tumor immune response.

It's pivotal study used RP1 in combo with nivolumab - Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) PD-1 inhibitor Opdivo, and achieved an objective response rate of ~33%, however the FDA apparently concluded the study has enrolled too broad a patient population, making it hard to assess results, and was also unhappy with the design of a "confirmatory" study that Replimune intended to conduct post-approval, to confirm its Phase 1/2 data.

Replimune shares the following overview of IGNYTE study results as follows in its latest investor presentation:

As the CEO explained back in January, the company had confidence that it was RP1, not the previously administered PD-1 therapy that was doing the "heavy lifting:

So one of the important questions you always get when you're doing a single-arm trial, particularly with the combination is well, what is RP1 contributing? And I mentioned one of the ways we got to that was this very strict criteria so that we know that further PD-1-based treatments really wouldn't do very much, as I said, about 5%. So when you see about a 34% response rate, we know that the RP1 is really contributing.

At the time of the first rejection, there was widespread surprise at the FDA's rejection, with some blaming the new regime at the FDA - notably, Dr Vinay Prasad, the former Director of the Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research ("CBER"). With Prasad set to leave the FDA at the end of this month, however, it seems the FDA remains broadly unconvinced by Replimune's data set, and does not believe the updated data and analytics provided since a Type A meeting last year materially changes the case for approval.

The reality is, oncolytic viruses have struggled to prove their mettle both in the clinic (RP1 formerly failed a study in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma), and commercially - Amgen's Imlygic, approved to treat melanoma in 2015, likely earns <$100m per annum (Amgen does not break out its revenue contribution but would likely do so if the figure was >$100m).

Replimune - What Happens Now?

Announcing its fiscal Q3 earnings in February this year, Replimune reported a net loss of $(247m), up from $(182m) in the prior year period, and a cash position of $269m.

Replimune's plans have always extended beyond approval in advanced melanoma to indications addressing ~130k cancer patients, as illustrated in the slide below:

Doubtless, Replimune management will feel hard done by today and will want to press ahead with studies, but seemingly, with a cash position of $269.1M and a quarterly cash burn of $70.9M, its funding runway may only extend into the first quarter of 2027. It's likely Replimune would have looked to raise funds via a stock offering if its BLA had been approved today, at a much higher price than the current share price value of <$5, to fund a commercial launch, but it may now be challenging for the company to secure funding, or even issue debt.

In terms of the objective response rates ("ORRs") and Complete Response Rates ("CRs") observed in the IGNYTE study, they do seem broadly comparable to Amtagvi. However, Amtagvi seems to have performed well in the commercial setting - five-year analysis of the pivotal study of Amtagvi in melanoma patients revealed an ORR of 31.4%, a five-year overall survival ("OS") of 19.7%, a median duration of response ("mDor") of 36.5 months, and a median OS of 13.9 months.

Ultimately, however clinical studies are rarely comparable, patients have contrasting profiles, may respond differently to a particular therapy, and trial designs and outcomes must be crystal clear and pre-agreed with the regulatory authorities. Rightly or wrongly, the FDA was seemingly convinced by Iovance's data submission, and unconvinced by Replimune's.

At the time of writing, Replimune has not released any communications round the FDA's decision - management will doubtless believe they had reached agreement with the administration on the data required to secure approval, and supplied it, but the final decision always rests with the administration (for better or worse).

As such, it is hard to know how the company will react. The company's market cap has sunk to <$400m, and it may be the case Replimune will need to scale back operations and reprioritize resources - it would not necessarily be surprising to see a corporate restructuring, in order to reduce costs.

The post-confirmatory study in advanced melanoma is surely no longer required, and in many senses it feels as though Replimune is back at square one. The company does have a second program, RP2, currently progressing through a Phase 2/3 study in metastatic uveal melanoma, and I wonder if management will opt to prioritize this over RP1 going forward. Management says that:

The primary endpoints of the trial are overall survival and progression free survival, and key secondary endpoints are overall response rate and disease control rate. Phase 2/3 transition is expected in Q1 2027, with PFS analysis potentially supporting accelerated approval.

Concluding Thoughts - A Tough Day For Replimune, A Quick Turnaround In Fortunes Feels Unlikely

My take on today's news is that the FDA has doubled down on the criticism of Replimune's pivotal study it shared with its first CRL, and does not believe its criticisms have been addressed by the re-submission.

Facing its second rejection, my take would also be that Replimune cannot have too many complaints. Oncolytic viruses, while a promising potential therapy, have defied the best efforts of several pharmas and biotechs to devise an effective and safe therapy, and there did not seem to be enough signs that RP1 would have been a drastic improvement on Amtagvi, or potentially even PD-1 plus chemotherapy, while other melanoma therapies - Moderna's (MRNA) / Merck's (MRK) cancer "Vaccine" candidate for example, may soon provide another treatment option in a field of admittedly high unmet need.

For Replimune, its feels as though it is back to the drawing board, and management may need to take some tough decisions. It will certainly be interesting to see the company's response, but whatever it may be, personally, and doubtless many readers will disagree, this does not feel like a "buy the dip" moment for Replimune stock.

data.title: Invivyd: Why The Trial Upsizing Isn't The Green Light Bulls Think It Is
data.description:
Invivyd Overview

Invivyd, Inc. (IVVD) is gaining some attention after providing an update on its COVID-19 trial evaluating its experimental monoclonal antibody, VYD2311.

I’ll admit that I was a bit surprised by the stock’s positive reaction. Basically, the DECLARATION study got upsized, which means it’s running slower and/or weaker than hoped. Given the decline in COVID-19 cases, this was somewhat expected. To prove VYD2311 works, a minimal number of events need to happen.

I last covered Invivyd in November. The article followed Merck’s acquisition of Cidara, which, like Invivyd, was, too, developing long-acting infectious disease drugs. However, I cautioned that the readthrough was limited. Cidara’s focus was on a stable and established influenza market. Whereas Invivyd targets a much smaller and more uncertain COVID-19 market. I also thought that VYD2311 is an incremental improvement over its current COVID-19 monoclonal antibody product, Pemgarda.

I ended with a Sell rating on IVVD. Its stock has since dropped ~33%.

I take another look below.

Recent Developments

Although Invivyd noted that DECLARATION “can already provide sufficient statistical power to support the high end of anticipated VYD2311 efficacy," it is adding 500 subjects (~28% expansion). This basically admits that if the drug works really well, the current dataset is fine. The upsizing implies Invivyd doesn’t want to bet on the “high end,” which is the safe bet. This update will result in a ~2-month delay.

So this isn’t necessarily a “bad” thing. But it is almost certainly not a “good” thing. There’s also a possibility for another delay, which could push VYD2311 back an entire “season.”

For the most part, VYD2311 remains “on track” to replace Pemgarda (see pipeline), which currently has EUA from the FDA. Invivyd is also developing VYD2311 for “Long COVID,” for which no treatment exists.

Within the same press release, Invivyd also revealed a new monoclonal antibody candidate for Measles, VMS063.

The science sounds legit. VMS063 targets the same validated mechanism (F protein pre-fusion conformation) as Beyfortus in RSV.

But it’s very early (preclinical discovery with IND-enabling studies not yet complete). It’s also risky and, potentially, costly. Invivyd cited three use cases (treatment, passive prophylaxis, and early childhood bridging), which could involve separate labels, trials, and commercial strategies.

Moreover, the company frames VMS063 as potentially replacing vaccination for hesitant populations, which is incongruent with current guidelines/recommendations (e.g., CDC). So it’s as if Invivyd is also hoping to create an entirely new market that, ideally (if people followed traditional public health mandates), should not exist. This could attract regulatory scrutiny if Invivyd markets a product as a way to “delay” or “supplement” a highly successful public health tool like the MMR vaccine.

During the call, management described mRNA vaccine antigens as “fundamentally toxic.” This explicitly courts people who are fearful of mRNA technology. Invivyd's “rebel” branding can go both ways. Sure, it may please some investors, but it could also make the regulatory path more challenging.

I touched on this in my last analysis, but an interesting risk is that COVID-19 will likely continue to evolve and, eventually, make VYD2311 “obsolete.” In fact, one immunologist even went on record in declaring this.

Invivyd counters by claiming they have possibly engineered a monoclonal antibody that “never requires updating.” This would be quite an accomplishment because there are a handful of antibodies that have since been laid to waste after COVID-19 evolved.

And what of the demand for a COVID-19 monoclonal antibody?

You can look no further than Invivyd itself. Its marketed monoclonal antibody, Pemgarda, generated just $53.4M in net product revenue last year. It is primarily a “specialty” medicine because it is authorized only for the immunocompromised population.

It’s not apples-to-apples, though. Pemgarda is administered intravenously. VYD2311 is intramuscular (a simple shot). And it is specifically being tested in the DECLARATION study for a “broad population,” including healthy adults and adolescents. Invivyd is hoping to compete directly with mRNA vaccines for the general public. In fact, another trial, LIBERTY (expected to initiate in Q2), plans to see VYD2311 go head-to-head against mRNA vaccines to compare safety and “reactogenicity” (i.e., fever, aches).

But, again, to play devil’s advocate, monoclonal antibodies are expensive. Payers may not be willing to cover a $1k monoclonal antibody for prophylaxis for a “healthy” person when an mRNA vaccine is available for <$100.

Invivyd’s foray into Measles, now in addition to RSV (preclinical), is an attempt to pivot away from “niche COVID-19 player” to an "infectious disease difference-maker." While laudable, this must be viewed in the context of its current financial state.

Financial Health

In its latest 10-K filing, Invivyd expressed “substantial doubt” as a “going concern.”

Based on our current operating plans and excluding any contribution from future revenues or external financing, however, we believe that our existing cash and cash equivalents will not be sufficient to fund our operating expenses and capital expenditure requirements for more than one year from the issuance of the consolidated financial statements for the year ended December 31, 2025.

Invivyd ended 2025 with $226.7M in cash and cash equivalents (after a >$200M raise in the 2H). "Cash burn" (cash from operations) in Q4 was $15.5M.

Assuming stable OpEx, this gives Invivyd ~3 years of cash runway. However, investors should expect increased cash burn in 2026 due to the fact that Invivyd intends to run multiple expensive studies at once (DECLARATION, LIBERTY, SPEAR for Long COVID, and IND-enablement and manufacturing for VMS063).

While I bring up the “going concern” doubt, I am not too worried (relative to other predominantly “pre-revenue” biotechs). They should have sufficient capital for the Q3 2026 readout and subsequent regulatory approval. I suspect that its ability to raise more money (advantageously or not) will lean heavily on these outcomes.

Conclusion

My biggest concern with Invivyd is not its clinical prospects. Like many antibodies before it, VYD2311 very well may prevent/treat whatever current versions of COVID-19 are out there. The FDA may green-light it. Invivyd may raise money at advantageous stock prices to remove its “going concern” doubt or secure an ex-U.S. partnership.

But, as I often warn in my analyses, clinical success does not guarantee a commercial blockbuster. Invivyd is fighting a two-front war against both biological evolution and established public health infrastructure. They are also burning through cash to reach a general public that has widely developed “COVID fatigue.” Basically, they are betting that it can change the behavior of millions of people who are either satisfied with current vaccines or finished with preventative shots entirely.

Recent developments do not calm my nerves on its stock (maintain “Sell”).

Risks to my thesis include clinical trial success (especially in Long COVID), regulatory approval, mRNA vaccine approval revocation, health misinformation, partnership, M&A interest, and VYD2311 durability.

data.title: The 3 Names You Need To Know To Invest In This Market
data.description:
Noise may be the rule of the markets currently. There are many points/trends that really impact companies, but at the same time, a lot of volatility and with a lot of information that changes all the time, basically the definition of “chaos”. Part of this is due to geopolitical factors, but I believe that a large part is also because of technological changes.

And even following very closely the technology sector, it is difficult to have a clear view of what can happen, with so many different opinions, trends and events. To understand a bit more, I believe that three names are very important, and some are already being very debated: Amara, Solow and Jevons.

They are thinkers that observed trends that we may be seeing happening right now, or at least, it serves as a theory of what can happen in the next few years. And putting the three together, I believe that what is formed is an understanding that even with these uncertainties, the scenario for the long run is bullish for the US stock market. After all, we will never (or almost never) pay good prices for good companies when the scenario is completely clear and certain.

Roy Amara and Sentiment

The first that I will discuss is what they call Amara’s Law. In a few words: we, human beings, overestimate the impact of technology in the short run and underestimate it in the long run.

The chart explaining this (expectations over time), looks like this:

It is possible to apply this theory in several technological disruptions, such as the internet and the dotcom bubble. It is also possible to apply more recently with software stocks, with companies trading at triple-digit P/Es (~100x+) and growing ~10% or 20%, this with a high dilution via stock-based comp (SBC), and a couple of months/years later a new “reality check”.

But obviously, I want to apply this to AI. Some years ago, Nvidia (NVDA) seemed very expensive, I believe that even today some investors must think that it is an expensive company. But actually, Nvidia is being traded for a fwd P/E just slightly higher than the S&P 500 (SPY) and below the Nasdaq (QQQ). I am saying this, to demonstrate that clearly we are no longer in the “peak of inflated expectations” with AI.

The market now starts to question the high CapEx of hyperscalers, the sustainability of demand for Nvidia GPUs, how much margin traditional companies will be able to expand with the new agents. And they are valid doubts. GPUs, memory, chips in general are quite expensive. Mainly, the CapEx really is in an enormous volume, it is not even possible to compare with other periods.

But as Amara’s law mentions, I think that the market is also underestimating the long-term, that is, there is a possibility of us being close to the “trough of disillusionment”. Tokens are so expensive now, AI infrastructure is so expensive now, AI does not always “get it right”, so it’s easy to think: “will an AI agent really have a positive ROI to the point of justifying a premium valuation?”

In my view, AI will indeed be something to change several industries. Actually, it already is. Think of Meta (META), for instance. The operating margin has been improving over the years, and mainly, the company managed to return to having excellent operational numbers. The price per ad, for instance, is a direct reflection of better use of AI, algorithms in general, and that its platforms are more robust.

So, in my view, the recent S&P 500/Nasdaq “dips”, may be coinciding with the Amara Dip, that is, the market is concerned about geopolitical uncertainties, but also for not seeing a growth as fast as it wanted, and not trusting so much in AI in the short term. This ends up making good companies with real potential in the long term be traded for very reasonable valuations, and consequently, the indexes that have a heavy weight in great companies, also become discounted.

And my optimism comes precisely from: I think that in the long term AI is still very promising. AI agents are something that is only starting.

I think the easiest way to think about this, maybe is thinking in traditional companies. Walmart (WMT) has a very physical structure, it is something still very intense in logistics and it is not what you think of when you think about an AI company probably, but look at this excerpt from the earnings call:

“The way we're using technology and AI is helping us create great customer solutions, reduce friction, simplify decision-making and pinpoint where our inventory is, all while maintaining the trust we've earned from our customers and members.”

Walmart has a relevant weight in the index, but it was just the first example that came to my mind, most of the companies of the S&P 500 will benefit. AI for pricing, better ads to increase sales, to automate repetitive tasks and consequently be able to expand margin and free up effort of people (human capital, if you will) to focus on more strategic tasks to expand top line and so on.

I am talking about in a general way, companies in consumer staples, health care, consumer cyclical, financials, even commodities. But there is also the other part that clearly benefits from this and has a high weight in Nasdaq/S&P 500. AI as a secular trend in the long term, meaning a stable demand for updates of Data Centers, and consequently more stable sales of Nvidia GPUs. Companies like Walmart developing several AI agents, also means directly a consumption of tokens, many times 24/7, that is, I am also bullish with hyperscalers like Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and even if there is a cyclicality in the next few years, I believe that the infrastructure that they are creating currently, will have an absurd demand.

Solow: Market Is Not Seeing ROI.. Yet

Well, our second name, Robert Solow, comes to explain a bit of the sentiment of ‘skepticism’ of the market with respect to ROIC and spending. The idea of Solow was that the entire world saw the “computer age” everywhere, but did not see it in productivity statistics.

Basically, the world adopted computers in a massive way, but until this came to appear in official productivity data, it took about 10 years, which is called the “Solow Paradox”. This basically explains that these large technological leaps take time to be implemented. You do not give a computer with Excel to your employee, and in the next month all your payrolls, suppliers, are already only in electronic sheets. It takes time for training, it takes time for implementation, it takes time for all stakeholders to get used to the change.

I think a very clear parallel is large M&A deals. Of course, there are companies that are experts in growing inorganically and make acquisitions and integrate without many problems. But if you, like me, have already seen large companies with a very large M&A, you know that much of what is expected ends up changing along the way. I covered some Brazilian companies that merged, and in the Powerpoint it seems simple, a lot of redundancy and $x billions to capture in synergies, x basis points of margin to improve in the next three years and everything else. But then comes the real test, real life. Different managers with different opinions, with different egos and different views for the same company. Hidden costs that were underestimated, different systems and platforms, contracts whose assumption was to be revised easily, now have fines or will take longer than expected to be revised, or even something more intangible like the culture that did not fit.

A concrete example that comes to my mind now is Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) with Paramount (PSKY), something ambitious, but that will need a very good execution to work in the “desired” time.

Note that I did not say that these M&A deals always go wrong, or that most of the time they go wrong, my point is not that.

My point is that many times people tend to overlook the difficulty of these things. AI agents may seem like something super easy to implement nowadays, a few days with an expert in Claude would already be enough to automate some tasks. But in practice it is much more complex than that. Think about banks that have a large physical exposure like Santander (SAN) that I covered recently. There are many branches, different types of data, legacy systems but that are in transition with digital, until all of this is consolidated so that an AI agent can really “enter” in the bank and automate, it is not in months, but in years.

This evidently applies to the companies of the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, and how this should impact the financials, but it is something to think also about the economy in general. Small companies will take time until they see that several repetitive tasks, like taking numbers from a paper to put in a spreadsheet, can be automated by an AI agent, saving a lot of time for their team and consequently increasing productivity.

Another point that I would like to highlight, is that even if it seems that the “ROIC” has not arrived yet or is too uncertain, the financials of the S&P 500 are far from being bad. The revenue continues to increase, and note how the operating margin grew a lot recently. There is a component of mix, since companies that gained more relevance in the index like Nvidia (NVDA) have a better margin, but there is also a component that most of these companies that are relevant in the index, improved their margin in the last few years (a lot of tech contribution). Maybe the market is still skeptical because of the amount, even if AI already gives results, it is difficult to have clarity of how this will behave when the CapEx is hundreds of billions.

Jevons And The “Infinite Demand”

I imagine that William Jevons is the most known of the names that I mentioned. At least it is the one that I see the most being talked about by journalists and analysts of the stock market/tech market. The theory is that, the more efficient a resource is, the greater the consumption/demand for it.

In the second section I mentioned that I imagine that one of the concerns is not only about the use of AI, but about the volume of CapEx. With so many billions being invested, are we not creating something that in three years, hyperscalers will not have an idle capacity?

It is really something to think about, and it is not something we can be 100% sure of. But I imagine that Jevons explains well here. The more efficient the data centers become, more tokens are generated, and more cheap tokens are generated. Really, nowadays, many of these tokens are used by companies that do not have a positive profit margin, like OpenAI (OPENAI). But it is already starting to be used by several companies, as I mentioned that is the case of Walmart.

Now, think the following: in five years, it is likely that tokens will be more efficient and cheaper (as it is possible to see in the trend of the image below, that I saw in this article of Jack Bowman). But more than that, AI will be something more efficient. AI agents will be easier to implement and more useful. What may be being used to automate repetitive tasks today, filter data or improve algorithms, may in a few years be something much superior, used for more complex tasks. Think in the movie industry, for instance. I do not think that a movie will be generated alone by AI without strong curation and with a creative team behind it. But in a few years many special effects, dubbing, all ends of the industry may be using AI, and with programs that consume tokens whether to generate a simple image, or with AI agents searching in real time the main trends or to optimize some ad insert in the movie, predicting some next trend. Just a few examples that come to my mind, to illustrate that the more efficient AI/tokens are, the greater will be the use.

This point ends up connecting a lot with the second thinker. We can think that there is still a lot to happen in the AI industry. The cost per token is falling as already mentioned, but the price in general still has a lot of evolving to do. The relationship between supply and demand eventually needs to stabilize, but this can take years and the market may not be realizing this. Several companies are investing heavily in infrastructure to support not only better components, but to be able to supply all this demand. This goes from GPUs and memories like the one of Micron (MU) to more simple things.

Bottom Line And Risks

Amara tells me that there is a strong psychological component here, Solow points that what the market really wants to see (certain, ROIC, free cash flow margin) may just take a bit longer to occur, and is not “gone” or something like that, and finally, Jevons shows that AI still has growth to capture, with an almost “infinite” demand.
