However, I need to mention some risks. The first and most obvious is, my thesis being “all wrong”. Let’s imagine, the Jevons effect maybe is not observed in AI, nor the one of Amara. So in 2035, what we see is an S&P 500 “more of the same”. Margins that did not evolve that much, top line similar to what it is now or with a slower growth, and companies like Microsoft and Amazon that invested billions and billions and in 10 years did not manage to transform this into cash flow. Certainly it is not my base scenario, I believe that even if we are a bit more pessimistic, all this infrastructure that is being built will not be totally wasted, even if AI flops.

Besides the lack of demand for AI and me and the market being overestimating the demand, maybe there is another point to prevent this from scaling as well as we expect. One of the crucial points is the price per token. Even if the price has already fallen, if we have negative surprises that disturb this trend, e.g., electricity price booming, or even oil prices (it impacts Data Centers). So in a scenario where oil remains at high levels, geopolitical uncertainties continue, not only the sentiment towards the market becomes bad, but it also becomes less viable that all companies use tokens massively.

Anyway, I think that the way to mitigate these risks is something that I mentioned in a recent article: time arbitrage. The next 12 months will see free cash flow pressure, demand uncertainty, and many geopolitical issues. Looking at the following years, it becomes easier to understand that the S&P 500 still has a lot of quality, and as the chart below shows, at a quite reasonable price (near the EMA 200d).

data.title: Texas Pacific Land: The $27B Bet That The Permian Becomes An AI Power Grid (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Pacific Land Corporation (TPL) has seen quite a level of volatility since I first reviewed it back in early October, as I gave it a "Hold" rating. It has since appreciated 20%, though the stock has seen a sharp downfall since its recent peak. I’ll dive into how the company has shifted operationally since and why the stock has been crashing as of late.

Current Dynamics

First, TPL has announced that it has signed a strategic partnership with Bolt Data & Energy, which is an AI infrastructure platform. This has positioned TPL’s surface land to host a large-scale data center campus, thus leveraging the company’s unique ability to provide the three essentials of AI computing that are land, energy, and water. Parallel to this, TPL has reinforced its core royalty business with a $450.7 million all-cash deal for around 17,306 net royalty acres, mainly in the Midland Basin. This was highly strategic, as at that time prices were in a drought, meaning that TPL was able to demonstrate the power of its self-funded countercyclical investment model. The transaction also standardized the acreage to a 1/8th royalty interest, significantly increasing the company’s exposure to future drilling activity by major operators in the basin.

The strategic agreement with Bolt Data & Energy has been one of the major shifts in the company’s business model since its conversion to a C-corp. It is essentially positioning the land as a host for closed-loop energy data hubs. This is forcing a re-rating in TPL’s stock as it aligns with the secular growth of the AI industry instead of only production in the Permian Basin. These hubs are highly innovative as they are designed to use on-site natural gas to power high-density GPU clusters, which allows the facilities to operate independently of the ERCOT. This is a critical selling point for operators who face power shortages and grid reliability issues that regular hubs are facing. The long-term goal for TPL is to build multiple such campuses across its 882,000-acre footprint, transforming the Permian into a global hub for AI compute.

Metric-wise, Q4 was relatively good for the firm, as it reached 37,500 boe/d, which is a slight increase over Q3. This operational growth was achieved even as the average realized price fell from $34.10 per Boe to $29.33 per Boe, sequentially illustrating again the countercyclical strength of the company’s increasing market capture and royalty scale.

On the macro side, the Permian has continued to defy the conventional logic behind the energy industry through the recent years as rigs have fallen by more than 21% Y/Y from a peak of over 300 to roughly 240, but the basin continues to set records thanks to the incredible gains in efficiency for drilling and completion. Though the basin is currently hitting a gas takeaway bottleneck, until pipelines like Blackcomb and Hugh Brinson add around 4.0 Bcf/d of takeaway capacity by the end of 2026, crude will likely see its production growth capped.

As a two-week ceasefire took effect in the Middle East, crude started to plummet by around 15% in a single session, reducing the current war premium. Though prices still remain elevated, TPL is highly sensitive to any swings, as 52% of its consolidated revenue is derived from O&G royalties. The relief rally that came from this was a disaster for TPL’s stock, as it crashed by over 16%, as another headwind came about as well.

This would be the passing away of Murray Stahl. You might wonder who he is and why the impact was so big, as on paper, he was “only” a board member for TPL, but he was also the CEO and Chairman of Horizon Kinetics, TPL’s largest shareholder. He was a visionary advocate for the company for decades and started buying when it was only a thinly traded trust owning some land in West Texas. The fear behind this is that this will create a messy leadership transition, plus it could jeopardize the massive stake in TPL, forcing a major stake to go back on the market. Though I believe that the reaction was overblown, as the firm directly implemented its succession plan with the two new co-CEOs and stated that it was fully committed to finishing what Stahl started.

Valuation

In terms of simple financial metrics, TPL is certainly not cheap with its P/E GAAP of 54.23, which is at the higher range of its peers. The firm also has an enormous EV/EBITDA at around 39.55. The heightened stock price is also causing the yield to be relatively low despite the incredible margins by the business, as it now stands at 1.7%. I also did a scenario analysis on the firm for a 12-month price target, though I believe that my crude estimates were low based on the situation in the Middle East, which only has a 2-week pause for the moment. Based on this, the probability-weighted target would reach $395.2 per share, but my bullish thesis would imply a $506 price target. Though the real underlying value behind the firm is its high-margin business model that continues to print cash.

I already covered the AI data center boom, so I’ll go through the next-generation water management as the firm is nearing completion of a 10K bpd R&D desalination facility in Orla, Texas. This freeze desalination technology aims to convert produced water into a managed resource for industrial use or data center cooling, providing a sustainable alternative to subsurface disposal. Otherwise, I expect the company to leverage its strong balance sheet into deals to consolidate its Permian asset footprint, especially if crude prices fall sharply.

Risks

TPL is expensive, and it was surfing on a war premium that might erode faster than expected, plus prices are already baking in significant future growth from the AI and desalination projects, leaving little room for error.

There is also the question behind Horizon Kinetics potentially new direction as its long-time CEO and visionary, Murray Stahl, passed away. There is a possibility that the firm unloads its substantial stake in TPL, creating too vast a supply on the market, tanking prices for other shareholders.

Conclusion

Texas Pacific Land has been undergoing a high-velocity transformation as it settled governance disputes as well as a strategic pivot to AI data centers and desalination, which have redefined the investment thesis behind the company. Its core royalty business remains excellent, but the valuation hinges on whether it can accelerate the commercialization of the technology assets. I believe that it can do it, and so does the market in a way. I upgrade my rating to a "Buy."

data.title: WD-40 Company: Slow And Steady Growth
data.description:
Today we are revisiting the WD-40 Company (WDFC). This remains one of our oldest positions and one of the first stocks our founder ever owned after receiving a few shares as a gift as a child. We were a lone Buy call on the name again in the fall of 2025. Since then, we have seen a nice rally in shares, but much of those gains have been given back. It is expensive for the muted growth being experienced. And in the last five years it has really gone nowhere, and aside from collecting a little yield from the dividend, it's been painful.

Though it is one of several hundred long-term "house positions" at our Investing Group, the underperformance does stand out. But having owned it for decades, we are not letting it go anytime soon. But the company has struggled some with operational costs and very slow growth over time.

One thing remains a constant, and that is that, yes, the stock on traditional valuation metrics has, in modern times, always been more expensive than the market. It may not be justified by the growth, but it has long been premium priced for whatever reason. The company has been through many economic cycles and over time remains a stable and reliable grower. Today, we check back in on its just-reported fiscal second-quarter earnings. Let us discuss.

Revenue Growth

The market has remained somewhat volatile, though we are seeing fundamental execution from the company. Shares have historically traded at a premium, and while a mid-30s FWD EPS valuation is high for slow and stable growth (mid-single-digit EPS growth), that is a bit of a quality tax, if you will, that investors pay for this name. That can change in the future, and certainly there is better growth and value elsewhere. As such, we are moving to a hold here, as we have held shares for many years. It is true that the company has immense pricing power and has no AI threat because you cannot, and as long as we have machinery and parts that need lubrication, the company will enjoy demand. We also like that the WD-40 company is aggressively pushing its Specialist line and expanding its footprint in Asia.

Sales in fiscal Q2 were $161.7 million, up a strong 11% from last year. It is important to note that currency had a major impact here. On a constant currency basis, total net sales would have increased 4% to $152.4 million. We were looking for a performance in the high $150 million range as reported, so this top-line figure was a small beat relative to our expectations. We continue to expect growth to be slow and stable. If you are concerned about valuation relative to growth, then pass on it. But we like it for long-term dividend growth.

Regional Breakdown Of Fiscal Q2 Sales

We also like to look at the regional performance. In fiscal Q2, there was growth across all three trade blocs, though the drivers varied by region. Overall, net sales by location for fiscal Q2 were 44% in the Americas, 40% in EIMEA, and 16% in the Asia-Pacific region.

Americas

Sales in the Americas increased 10%, driven primarily by an 11% increase in maintenance product sales. The standout was the United States, where net sales rose by $5.0 million, or 15%. This growth was fueled by higher volumes through online retailers and expanded distribution, paired with modest price increases. The WD-40 Specialist line also performed well here, with sales up 17% due to better product placement.

EIMEA

Over in EIMEA, sales increased $5.3 million, or 9%. This was largely driven by a 12% increase in maintenance product sales, though much of this was attributable to favorable currency exchange rates. While direct markets saw volume growth, distributor markets were a bit softer. We also saw a decline in home care and cleaning products here, which was expected following the divestiture of the U.K. portfolio last year. On a constant currency basis, this region actually saw a 3% decline.

Asia-Pacific

Over in the Asia-Pacific region, we saw the most significant percentage gains. Q2 sales surged 19% to $25.0 million. This was led by a 21% increase in maintenance products, particularly in China, where sales rose 18% thanks to promotional timing and expanded distribution. The Specialist brand is really taking off here, with sales up a massive 55%. This reflects a strong rebound after the normalization of inventory levels we saw in previous quarters.

Margins

Margins have been improving, which is the core of the bull case for holding the stock long-term. Gross margin in fiscal Q2 was 55.6%, up 100 basis points from 54.6% a year ago. This is strong, folks, and it shows that supply chain initiatives are bearing fruit. While operating income rose 13% to $26.3 million, net income was $20.3 million, a 32% decrease from last year.

However, don't let that headline number scare you. Last year’s quarter included an $11.9 million one-time tax benefit. If we exclude that, adjusted net income actually rose 13%. On the bottom line, EPS came in at $1.50. When comparing it to last year’s adjusted EPS of $1.32, we are looking at a solid 14% increase, which beat our expectations.

Fiscal 2026 Outlook And Management Comments

We think the outlook for the rest of the year is positive, and management reaffirmed their full-year guidance. Steve Brass, president and chief executive officer of WD-40 Company, stated during the call:

"(Our) gross margin also remained solidly within our expected guidance range for fiscal 2026, expanding 100 basis points in the quarter. Recent supply chain initiatives are supporting gross margin in the near term, giving us time to take mitigating actions as needed... With a number of growth initiatives planned across the business and momentum building across all three trade blocs, we are confidently reaffirming our full-year fiscal 2026 guidance."

That is a positive view. We like the margin profile. We note that the company is bracing for higher oil prices ahead, so if those are lower, that could be a benefit if there is some reprieve. We also love the commitment to share repurchases, with $13.8 million still available under the current authorization, following $8 million of repurchases this past quarter. Looking ahead, the company sees sales growth of 5% to 9% ($630 million to $655 million) and full-year margins between 55.5% and 56.5%.

Overall, EPS is expected at a midpoint of approximately $5.95, about 8% growth for the fiscal year. With accelerated repurchases and a growing dividend, along with steady maintenance product demand, we view WDFC stock as good for a long-term Hold here.
